entries.title: Testing mobile applications: overview
entries.body:
# Testing mobile applications with Burp

Burp can test not only websites in a browser but also the traffic of mobile applications. The idea is exactly the same as on the web: make the device push its HTTP(S) traffic through the Burp proxy. As soon as the traffic goes through Burp, you will be able to intercept, view, and modify all requests and responses of the mobile application and work with the familiar tools - **Proxy**, **Repeater**, **Intruder**, the scanner.

The general scheme:

```
[ Phone/emulator ] --Wi-Fi proxy--> [ Burp on PC :port ] --> [ Application server ]
        |                                    |
     proxy in Wi-Fi settings            Proxy > HTTP history
     + Burp CA installed                Intercept, Repeater, scan
```

For this to work you need two things:

1. **Proxy.** Configure on the device a proxy pointing at the IP of the computer with Burp and the port of the proxy listener. The phone and the computer must be on the same Wi-Fi network.
2. **Certificate.** Install the Burp CA certificate on the device, otherwise HTTPS traffic will break with certificate errors.

From here the documentation splits across platforms:

- configuring an iOS device;
- configuring an Android device;
- a troubleshooting section for common problems.

> Important: honestly intercepting the HTTPS of a mobile application can be HARD. The two main traps are (a) the application does not use the system proxy and (b) **certificate pinning**, where the application strictly checks the server's certificate and refuses to trust the Burp CA. Simple proxy and certificate configuration is enough for many applications, but not for all - for pinned ones you need separate techniques (see troubleshooting and **Mobile Assistant** for iOS).

> Pitfall: HTTP(S) only. It is precisely HTTP(S) traffic that the Burp proxy catches. If the application communicates over its own binary protocol on top of TCP/UDP, you will not see it in **Proxy > HTTP history** - for that you need other tools.

entries.title: Configuring an iOS device for Burp
entries.body:
# Configuring an iOS device

To test an iOS application, you need to route the iPhone/iPad traffic through Burp and install the trusted Burp CA on the device. The computer with Burp and the device must be on the same Wi-Fi network.

## Step 1. A proxy listener on all interfaces

By default Burp listens only on `127.0.0.1` - that is not enough for a phone, you need a listener on all interfaces:

1. Open **Settings > Tools > Proxy**.
2. In the **Proxy listeners** section click **Add**.
3. On the **Binding** tab: **Bind to port** = `8082` (or another free port), **Bind to address** = **All interfaces**.
4. **OK**. To the warning about external availability answer **Yes**.

```
Settings > Tools > Proxy > Proxy listeners > [Add]
  Binding:
    Bind to port:    8082
    Bind to address: (*) All interfaces
```

> Find out the IP of the computer on this Wi-Fi network (for example, `192.168.1.50`) - you will need it on the phone. On Windows this is `ipconfig`, on macOS/Linux - `ifconfig`/`ip addr`.

## Step 2. Proxy on iOS

1. On the device: **Settings > Wi-Fi**, connect to the same network.
2. Tap the information icon **(i)** next to the network name.
3. **Configure Proxy** -> **Manual**.
4. **Server** = the IP of the computer with Burp (for example, `192.168.1.50`).
5. **Port** = the listener port (`8082`).
6. **Save**.

Now HTTP traffic should already appear in **Proxy > HTTP history**. But HTTPS will still complain about the certificate for now - the next step fixes that.

## Step 3. Installing the Burp CA on iOS

1. Make sure Burp is running.
2. In the Safari browser on the device open `http://burpsuite`.
3. Click **CA Certificate** (top right) - the certificate profile will download.
4. iOS will offer: **Settings > Profile Downloaded** -> open it.
5. On the profile installation screen click **Install** (it will ask several times - confirm).
6. It is IMPORTANT to enable full trust: **Settings > General > About > Certificate Trust Settings** and switch the toggle for **PortSwigger CA** to the on position.

```
Safari -> http://burpsuite -> CA Certificate (download the profile)
Settings > Profile Downloaded > Install
Settings > General > About > Certificate Trust Settings
   [ON] PortSwigger CA   <- without this step HTTPS will not work
```

> Pitfall: the **Certificate Trust Settings** item is the one most often forgotten. Installing the profile is NOT ENOUGH - until you enable full trust for the PortSwigger CA in this section, iOS will not trust the certificate for HTTPS sites, and the traffic will keep breaking.

## Step 4. Verification

Turn on **Proxy > Intercept** and open any HTTPS site on the device. If it loads without security warnings and the requests are visible in Burp - the setup works.

> Pitfall: certificate pinning. Even with a correctly installed and trusted CA, many applications will refuse to work because they check a specific server certificate. The browser meanwhile works fine, but the target application does not. For pinned applications you need separate bypasses; **Burp Suite Mobile Assistant** partly helps with this (it tries to bypass pinning in selected applications).

entries.title: Configuring an Android device for Burp
entries.body:
# Configuring an Android device

An Android application can be tested just like an ordinary website: route the device's traffic through Burp and install the Burp CA. The details vary slightly depending on the device/emulator and the Android version, but the general scheme is the same. The device and the computer are on the same Wi-Fi network.

## Step 1. A proxy listener on all interfaces

1. Open **Settings > Tools > Proxy**.
2. **Proxy listeners** -> **Add**.
3. The **Binding** tab: **Bind to port** - any free port, **Bind to address** = **All interfaces**.
4. **OK**.

Remember the IP of the computer on this network - you need it to configure the proxy on the phone.

## Step 2. Proxy on Android

1. Go to the device's network settings, open the properties of your Wi-Fi network (often: long tap on the network -> **Modify network**).
2. Enable showing the additional options (**Advanced options**).
3. **Proxy** -> **Manual**.
4. **Proxy hostname** = the IP of the computer with Burp.
5. **Proxy port** = the listener port from step 1.
6. Save and reconnect to the network.

```
Wi-Fi > (network) > Modify network > Advanced options
  Proxy:          Manual
  Proxy hostname: 192.168.1.50
  Proxy port:     8080
```

## Step 3. Installing the Burp CA on Android

Export the certificate from Burp in DER format:

1. **Settings > Tools > Proxy** -> **Import / export CA certificate**.
2. **Export > Certificate in DER format**.
3. Save the file with the extension `.der`.
4. Transfer the file to the device and add it to the trusted certificates.

(The certificate can also be downloaded right on the device by opening `http://burpsuite` in the browser -> **CA Certificate**; the file will arrive as `cacert.der`.)

## The main trap: Android 7 (Nougat) and above

This is the key point specifically for Android. Starting with **Android 7 (Nougat)**, applications BY DEFAULT do NOT trust user-installed CA certificates. That is, you can install the Burp CA as a user certificate - the browser will trust it, but the target application will ignore it and HTTPS will break.

For an application to trust the Burp CA on Android 7+, the certificate needs to be placed in the SYSTEM store, and for that a **rooted device** is required (or an emulator with privileges). The alternative without root - if you have the application's source code - is to rebuild it with a network security config that allows trusting user certificates. But this is applicable only to your own build, not to someone else's installed APK.

```
Android < 7:  user CA -> applications trust it. It is enough to install the Burp CA.
Android >= 7: user CA -> applications do NOT trust it by default.
              you need root + moving the CA to the system store,
              or rebuilding the APK with network_security_config.
```

> Pitfall: rooting reduces the device's security and may void the warranty. For testing one usually uses a separate test phone or an emulator, not a personal device.

## Step 4. Verification

Turn on **Proxy > Intercept**, open an HTTPS site on the device - it should load without warnings and appear in **Proxy > HTTP history**.

> Pitfall: as on iOS, **certificate pinning** is able to block interception even with a correctly installed system CA. For pinned Android applications one applies separate bypass techniques (for example, instrumenting the application with third-party tools) - that is already beyond the standard Burp setup.

entries.title: Mobile: troubleshooting
entries.body:
# Troubleshooting when testing mobile applications

Mobile interception breaks more often than web, and the causes are usually from a short list. Let us go through the typical ones.

## HTTPS does not open on iOS even with the Burp CA installed

This is a specific problem described in the documentation. The symptom: the certificate seems installed and trusted, but HTTPS sites on the device still do not load, while in **Dashboard > Event log** a pile of errors of the form `Connection reset` or `Remote host terminated the handshake` keeps appearing.

The cause is a TLS 1.3 incompatibility. The solution: disable TLS 1.3 for the proxy listener serving the device.

1. **Settings > Tools > Proxy**.
2. Select your device's proxy listener -> **Edit**.
3. The **TLS Protocols** tab.
4. Select **Use custom protocols**.
5. Uncheck **TLSv1.3**.

```
Symptom: HTTPS on iOS does not go through, in Event log - Connection reset / handshake terminated
Fix:     Settings > Tools > Proxy > (listener) > Edit > TLS Protocols
         (*) Use custom protocols
         [ ] TLSv1.3   <- uncheck
```

After this HTTPS usually starts working, and the stream of errors in the log dies down. If it did not help - it makes sense to contact PortSwigger support.

## Other typical causes (general practice)

There are few of these items on the page itself, but in practice mobile interception most often comes down to one of:

- **Traffic does not appear in Burp at all.** Check that the listener listens on **All interfaces** (and not just `127.0.0.1`), that the correct computer IP and port are entered in the phone's Wi-Fi settings, and that the device and the computer are on the SAME network. A frequent stopper is a firewall/antivirus on the PC blocking incoming connections to the Burp port: the inbound rule needs to be allowed.

- **HTTP goes through, but HTTPS breaks.** That means the problem is in the certificate. On iOS double-check that trust is enabled in **Certificate Trust Settings**. On Android 7+ recall that applications do not trust the user certificate - you need a CA in the system store (root) or a rebuild with a network security config.

- **The browser on the phone works, but the target application does not.** A classic sign of **certificate pinning**: the application strictly checks the server certificate and rejects the Burp CA, even though the system trusts it. This is not cured by the standard setup - you need separate pinning bypasses (for iOS **Mobile Assistant** partly helps; on Android - instrumenting the application with third-party tools).

- **The application ignores the system proxy.** Some applications do not use the proxy from the Wi-Fi settings and go directly. Then the traffic cannot be routed past Burp with a simple setup - you have to redirect it at the network level (for example, via invisible proxying / routing rules) or instrument the application.

> Diagnostic tip: first of all always look at **Dashboard > Event log**. Connection errors, TLS handshake failures, and messages about certificates Burp honestly writes there - this immediately narrows the range of causes from "unclear why it does not work" down to a specific class of problem.
